metadata.title: Taking sides: Corporate social responsibility and political ideology
keywords: domestic political economy, US political economy
corporate political activity Alt. env. CSR, alternative environmental CSR

abstract: In the United States and other countries, large private firms are increasingly more likely to take public political stances on controversial issues. Firms behave as ideological actors when they take sides in large public debates on social and economic issues. These stances may involve public speech, changes in the terms of service, or in internal reorganization and intrafirm actions. Despite the increase in firms' political actions, firms' ideological orientations remain unexamined. To answer this question, we collect corporate social responsibility statements from Fortune 1000 firms. Using semisupervised topic models, we identify topics that reflect stances on environmental and social issues. We then examine if firms are more likely to take stances on political issues due to pragmatic or ideological factors. We find that while pragmatic considerations play a role, firms' political stances are more driven by the ideological orientation of employees and managers. This research contributes a novel measure of firm ideology and sheds new light on the determinants of corporate political activity.

sections.paper_id: Gur and Tomashevskiy - 2024 - Taking sides Corporate social responsibility and political ideology
paragraphs: In recent years, large corporations have been more willing to take public stances on hot-button political issues in the United States and elsewhere. These issues include public movements such as Black Lives Matter, climate change, LGBT+ issues, Brexit, and others. This appears to be a departure from conventional types of corporate political activity. While less visible corporate political activity such as lobbying or political contributions may display political preferences, large firms have typically abstained from taking public stances on controversial issues. Recent actions by firms thus represent a change in behavior in that firms are becoming more visible and active as organizations with particular political preferences.

In this paper, we focus on public messaging, a type of corporate political activity (CPA) that has received less attention in existing research (Yoffie & Bergenstein, 1985). This type of activity is different compared to other, less public forms of CPA, such as monetary contributions, which are more directed toward a specific political goal. Public communication can be a type of cheap talk: firms may communicate commitments toward some set of values without actually changing their behavior or policies. Nevertheless, political communication may be costly if firms alienate potential customers with opposing political views and values. Therefore, it is not clear why firms advance these potentially costly signals to public audiences. As highly influential economic actors, the type of political stances adopted by firms may play a role in broader political conflicts and societal trends.

What explains this type of CPA? Existing research posits two primary motivations for CPA: pragmatic and ideological (Hansen & Mitchell, 2000;McMenamin, 2012;Mitchell et al., 1997). The pragmatic view has been a primary focus of much existing work on CPA. Firms act pragmatically to take political stances that are likely to maximize profits and contribute to positive public perception (Bostdorff & Vibbert, 1994). The pragmatic view assumes that firms as organizations have no particular political preferences and thus hedge their political activity (McMenamin, 2012). Hedging may manifest as a willingness to switch party loyalties when providing campaign contributions, or by shifting political stances to adjust to the prevailing attitudes among consumers. To the extent that some companies take public stances on political issues, they do so to maximize profits and thus pragmatically adopt stances that correspond to broad societal preferences. Pragmatic motivations may include strategies to improve performance on some set of third party rankings and measures, a desire to build broad social legitimacy among consumers and societal stakeholders, or public relations strategies aimed at offsetting some negative event such as corporate criminal prosecution. A firm's reputation is integral to long-term wealth creation, and thus firms have pragmatic incentives to advance certain social and political issues to boost positive perception among shareholders and societal stakeholders. These incentives are particularly pressing for companies that have recently been the target of criminal prosecution, as they suffer from negative reputation costs (Bhagwat et al., 2020;O'Connor, 2006;Williams & Barrett, 2000).

Alternatively, companies may take political stances for ideological reasons (Ansolabehere et al., 2003;Bonica, 2016;Chin et al., 2013;Gupta et al., 2017;Maks-Solomon & Drewry, 2021;Neustadtl & Clawson, 1988). Since organizations such as firms are made up of individuals, the overall political orientation of a firm is a reflection of the preferences of its employees and managers. CPA such as contributions to politicians may be motivated by the ideological beliefs of managers and employees of the firm. Firms' political messaging and position-taking are then ideologically motivated, rather than strategically motivated. Rather than hedging, firms as organizations possess ideological and partisan preferences that are reflected in the firm's willingness to take stances on political issues.

Firms' public political positions are then not motivated by some pragmatic goal but are instead shaped by larger political conflicts and cleavages in society. These political positions are reflections of broader political outcomes viewed as desirable by individuals wielding power in the firm (Bonica, 2016;Maks-Solomon & Drewry, 2021).

These two competing explanations have not been adequately resolved in the existing literature. While evidence exists in favor of both perspectives, existing work has not directly examined the determinants of firms' political positions. Although other types of CPA, such as campaign contributions or lobbying expenditures, are more easily measured and quantified, less useful data exists to permit inferences concerning firms' stances on specific social and political issues. In this paper, we attempt to resolve this issue and examine Fortune 1000 firms' political stances using corporate social responsibility (CSR)/environmental, social, and governance (ESG) statements. These statements play an important role in firms' public communications and thus represent a useful data source to measure political preferences. We use semisupervised topic modeling on CSR statements from Fortune 1000 firms to examine how firms communicate stances on social and environmental issues. Using a document's proportion devoted to social and environmental topics as the dependent variable, we evaluate how pragmatic and ideological variables explain variation across CSR statements. We find that ideological factors, measured by employees' political contributions, play a larger role compared to pragmatic factors, such as criminal prosecutions in the United States. We find that firms with more liberal employees are more likely to communicate left-oriented social messages. Conversely, the recent criminal prosecution of the firm does not affect social messaging. This suggests that corporate political stances are motivated more by the ideological beliefs of managers and employees, rather than by pragmatic considerations designed to maximize profit. Counterintuitively, we find that firms with more liberal employees devote less time to environmental messaging, suggesting that social and environmental messages are substitutes rather than complements. These results provide novel evidence concerning the role of ideological factors across different issue areas.

This paper contributes to larger debates on the determinants of CPA (Hansen & Mitchell, 2000;Mitchell et al., 1997). If ideological factors matter, firms may engage in political activity to the detriment of possible profits and market share. From an organizational perspective, this suggests that organizations may become more outwardly ideological when composed of individuals with similar ideological beliefs (Gupta et al., 2017). These findings contribute to long-standing debates in political economy research on the motivations of corporate political activity. Our results indicate that CPA is in part motivated by political ideology, not profit-maximizing behavior. The political activities of firms are thus impacted by political cleavages and conflicts in wider society. This finding has broader implications for other types of CPA and the role of firms in politics.

We proceed as follows. First, we discuss the literature on corporate political activity and examine the ideological and pragmatic determinants of CPA. Next, we present and describe our new CSR dataset. We then analyze these data using keyword-assisted topic modeling and discuss the results. We conclude by considering some implications for future research.

sections.paper_id: Gur and Tomashevskiy - 2024 - Taking sides Corporate social responsibility and political ideology
paragraphs: Corporations engage in political actions through a variety of formal and informal activities. Formal corporate political activities include direct monetary contributions to electoral candidates or lobbying to gain access to legislature and influence regulatory frameworks.

These activities are generally aimed at shaping the regulatory environment, promoting favorable policy outcomes, or supporting politicians through institutionalized means. On the other hand, companies also engage in more direct public messaging through CSR and participation in ESG frameworks. These public messages convey firms' self-regulatory activities such as climate change adaptation and mitigation, as well as their stances on issues related to diversity and inclusion of marginalized and underrepresented groups. Through these frameworks, firms convey their positions on contentious and partisan social and environmental issues.

The rapidly rising number of CSR frameworks integrated into big businesses is a relatively new phenomenon (Steinberg, 2021). Globalization reduced barriers to trade and investment, and increased foreign direct investment has substantially widened the number of areas impacted by business operations. These operations and their social and environmental impacts caused a public backlash toward businesses. Non-governmental organizations and other civil society organizations pressured firms to take progressive action on social, economic, and environmental issues. Thus, CSR frameworks became a common feature of big business strategies (Detomasi, 2008). CSR broadly refers to a practice in which firms voluntarily provide social, environmental, and economic goods and services to communities affected by their operations (Steinberg, 2021). More specifically, a widely accepted definition posits that "CSR is the configuration of the principles of social responsibility, processes of social responsiveness, policies, programs, and observable outcomes as they relate to the firm's societal relationships" (Wood, 1991). CSR frameworks thus form an important but overlooked aspect of firms' political activities, as these frameworks often reflect firms' positions in larger political cleavages.

While CSR is ostensibly apolitical, some CSR directions, related to broader societal debates on race, gender, and the environment, are aligned more closely with the political left. In part, this pattern is the outcome of left-wing preferences of civil society and progressive organizations that pressure large corporations. Another aspect of this pattern is the widespread acceptance of traditionally liberal ideals, especially among high-income individuals representing important customer groups. This change impacts both the beliefs of employees and managers, as well as the societal expectations and constraints on company practices. While companies often pursue a variety of political strategies in less visible contexts, such as campaign contributions, CSR statements, and public communications generally emphasize issues associated with the left.

Existing literature provides some explanations for these seemingly costly strategies: (i) pragmatic motivations and (ii) ideological factors. Pragmatic motivations describe firms' incentives to countervail negative public image, advance their market share and profits, hedge sectoral and social risks, comply with regulatory/institutional pressures, and attract a skilled labor force (Briscoe & Safford, 2008;Detomasi, 2008;Midttun et al., 2007;Pacheco & Dean, 2015). First, in some cases, companies pursue CSR strategies to mitigate negative public backlash and countervail unpopular business practices through civil society initiatives. Firms thus pragmatically advance CSR strategies to minimize the impact of environmental or social prosecution/scandals on consumption patterns. For example, Greenpeace successfully pressured Shell to halt the deep-sea disposal of the Brent Spar oil platform and convinced Shell to dispose of it onshore (Detomasi, 2008). Similarly, negative public backlash toward footwear and apparel company Nike over its subcontractor production arrangements that resulted in poor working conditions has pressured the company to expand its CSR programs (Kytle & Ruggie, 2005). However, reactive CSR strategies are not systematic and are less likely to change long-run firm behavior. In other cases, companies that have already been targeted by formal criminal prosecution in various issue areas may introduce new ESG initiatives to neutralize the negative reputation cost between different audiences. These might be related to both short-and long-term reputation-building strategies.

Therefore, CSR strategies may sometimes go beyond reactive efforts to offset some negative effects of corporate prosecution/scandals and be directed toward meeting broader expectations among consumers and societal stakeholders (Bhagwat et al., 2020;O'Connor, 2006;Williams & Barrett, 2000). These strategies might build reputation, ease employee recruitment, and build customer loyalty (Jensen, 2002;Windsor, 2001). Inglehart and Norris (2016) and Norris and Inglehart (2019) highlight that postindustrial societies experienced a progressive value change, in which new secular values such as LGBT+ rights, environmentalism, open-mindedness toward migrants, and cosmopolitanism became salient, particularly among more educated younger generations. Therefore, firms strategically advance social and environmental CSR programs to build a positive public image to ease high-skilled employee recruitment. Companies also adopt CSR to comply with institutional and regulatory pressures (Mobus, 2012;Ngoasong, 2014). Indeed, these voluntary initiatives cannot be understood in separation from other regulatory threats (Grepperud & Pedersen, 2003). Detomasi (2008) and others argue that the political environment in which a firm operates has specific implications for the firm's choice of whether or how to pursue CSR (Aguilera-Caracuel et al., 2017;Dilling & Harris, 2018;Jindrichovska & Purcarea, 2011;Liu et al., 2014).

This political and regulatory landscape can sometimes incentivize firms to adopt politicized CSR strategies. An example of such a strategy is the launching of CSR initiatives by governments themselves, like targeted poverty alleviation campaigns. These campaigns, often supported by state-owned enterprises, illustrate a collaboration where political goals align with CSR. Firms, especially those like local enterprises or state-owned entities with strong ties to the government, are more likely to engage in these state-led CSR activities. This engagement is particularly evident in countries like China, where state-owned enterprises are recognized for assuming extensive social responsibilities (Deng et al., 2024;Huang et al., 2024). Furthermore, CSR/ESG programs can extend beyond consumer perceptions of the firm and also influence how the public feels about the regulatory policy context companies operate in (Culpepper et al., 2024;Kerner & Sumner, 2020;Malhotra et al., 2019). This implies that companies can strategically advance CSR initiatives to control societal expectations about potentially costly formal regulation or to be part of the design of policy initiatives. Formal regulations such as corporate income taxes may be costly for firms as they lower a firm's capital investment and its total factor productivity by raising the cost of capital. They will reduce posttax investment returns and decrease the profitability of businesses (Cevik & Miryugin, 2022). Therefore, managers have incentives to evade taxes and other regulatory initiatives such as environmental policies ranging from carbon taxes to compliance fees.

Moreover, these long-term considerations should also be more salient for companies suffering from negative reputational costs due to recent criminal prosecution. This suggests that companies that have been frequent and recent targets of criminal prosecution are more likely to publicly dedicate themselves to providing social and environmental goods. If unaccounted for, these negative public sentiments can escalate into broad public demands for corporate regulation. Similarly, certain industries that collectively suffer from poor reputations due to the extractive nature of their operations have more pragmatic incentives to communicate ESG efforts to pre-empt potentially costly public regulation (Malhotra et al., 2019).

Second, firms can also advance self-regulatory CSR mechanisms on social and environmental issues for competitive reasons. This type of pragmatic engagement is particularly salient for environmental issues, as the energy transition opens up new competitive market spaces for green industries, such as renewable energy and sustainable agriculture industries. The existing literature has pointed out various pragmatic motivations for companies to lobby in favor of stringent environmental standards or advance CSR/ESG initiatives. These include maximizing market share, achieving first-mover advantage, and adjusting to regulatory environments (Aguilera-Caracuel et al., 2017;Dong & Xu, 2016;Genovese, 2021;Genovese & Tvinnereim, 2019;Kennard, 2020;Liu et al., 2014;Porter & van der Linde, 1995;Vogel, 1997). Firms may thus expect that stances taken on social and environmental issues as part of a CSR strategy may contribute to overall higher profits and firm value (Bashtovaya, 2014;Ekatah et al., 2011;Midttun et al., 2007;Plumlee et al., 2015;Pätäri et al., 2014;Sepúlveda & Mendizabal, 2011). On the other hand, in some cases government-introduced mandatory CSR disclosures decrease the profitability of firms. These effects are particularly salient for state-owned enterprises and environmental sectors. This evidence suggests that CSR spending is primarily driven by social and political factors such as state ownership and polluting industries (Chen et al., 2018). In line with the literature on pragmatic motivations of firms for taking public political stances through CSR initiatives, we consider the following hypotheses:
H1. Firms will place greater emphasis on social and environmental CSR due to pragmatic motivations.

The literature on pragmatic motivations has mostly focused on how companies react to their external environment and highlighted factors related to profitability, market share, hedging risk, compliance with regulatory/institutional frameworks, and actions of industry peers to explain why firms behave as social and environmental stewards. Nonetheless, pragmatic motivations do not adequately capture why companies take public political positions on politically controversial issues that may not necessarily reflect concerns over profitability and other market-related mechanisms. Other research thus emphasizes ideological factors related to the internal characteristics of corporations such as the political action committees (PACs) (Bonica, 2016), the ideological orientation of the executives and the CEOs (Chin et al., 2013), and the organizational ideology of the firm and partisan preferences of its employees (Endres & Panagopoulos, 2017;Gupta et al., 2017;Maks-Solomon & Drewry, 2021;Newman & Bartels, 2011).

A relatively nascent literature focused on alternative factors that go beyond market-based explanations has started to investigate ideological factors that may contribute to the firms' decisions to make public political statements on controversial social and environmental issues. Indeed, socially responsible firms engage in fewer tax avoidance practices (Lanis & Richardson, 2013), and existing voluntary practices such as the donation culture discourage tax avoidance (Ge et al., 2023). However, we know relatively less about how factors like political ideology within the firms affect the emergence of voluntary firm initiatives such as the CSR and ESG programs.

Therefore, some scholars have shifted the attention toward high-rank executives, managers, and top decision-makers ideologies to understand various forms of CPA (Bonica, 2016;Chin et al., 2013). Indeed, some argue that any firm-level explanation is incomplete without accounting for the preferences of the firm's leadership. Recent research found that individual contributions by corporate elites reflect more partisan ideological preferences than typically assumed. Indeed, PACs behave more strategically than ideologically while engaging in political activity compared to individual contributions (Bonica, 2016). Nonetheless, even PACs need to adapt their strategies to the partisan and ideological preferences of their donors/employees (Li, 2018) Other research on ideological motivations has investigated the role of political ideologies of managers and business leaders independent of the political agendas of PACs (Chin et al., 2013;Francia et al., 2005;Tetlock, 2000). Individuals, including people employed in both mid and top-level positions, engage in political activities primarily due to their personal partisan preferences. Tetlock (2000), using a firm-level managerial survey, found that there are significant differences between liberal and conservative managers. Liberal managers are more likely to support firms' societal engagements as they value multiple stakeholders, whereas conservative managers value property rights more and prefer governance models that prioritize shareholder wealth. A more recent study focusing on CEOs' partisan political preferences found that liberal CEOs will advance CSR more than conservative CEOs (Chin et al., 2013).

While the focus on top executives and CEOs is useful for understanding CPA, top executives do not select corporate political activity solely on their own. Instead, they also have incentives to abide by pre-existing organizational ideology and other initiatives endorsed by employees at various organizational levels, enhancing the legitimacy of the CEOs within the firm (Suchman, 1995). It is plausible that other ideological factors related to the entire organizational body of the firm may explain the CPA and advancements in social and environmental CSR initiatives (Gupta et al., 2017;Maks-Solomon & Drewry, 2021).

Organizational political ideology broadly refers to prevailing political beliefs among an organization's members. While few firms have employees with homogeneous political beliefs, a certain degree of homogeneity is expected as individuals select places where the current members and policies attract them. When these types of homogenizations arise, they tend to be self-reinforcing and consequential in shaping certain types of CPA such as CSR (Gupta et al., 2017;Schneider, 1987). Organizational political ideology can guide policy-making within the firm through motivated cognition and logic of appropriateness. Motivated cognition refers to individuals' proclivity to reflect their values in their decisions. In addition, employees will also consider how their values match their understanding of the organization's prevailing ideology (logic of appropriateness). These two mechanisms are likely to enhance each other and can serve as the link between ideology, policy, and content of CSR initiatives (Gupta et al., 2017;Maks-Solomon & Drewry, 2021).

It is also important to note that firm-level characteristics may also affect individuals' political preferences on specific policy issues. A rich political economy literature focuses on occupational status and employer characteristics to explain political preferences on a wide range of policy issues including redistribution, trade, and foreign investment (Iversen & Soskice, 2001;Kitschelt & Rehm, 2014;Owen & Johnston, 2017;Scheve & Slaughter, 2004, 2001). The evidence suggests that the employees of more productive firms are more likely to support free trade as they expect to benefit from the firm's success in some way (Lee & Liou, 2022). Indeed, Stuckatz (2022) shows that donations of the corporate PACs and employees are correlated with each other. However, there is also counterevidence suggesting that even the access-seeking corporate PACs are constrained by the political ideology of their employees/donors (Li, 2018).

Nonetheless, the distributional consequences of some issues such as free trade, redistribution, and investment might be relatively easier to identify for employees compared to social and environmental policies related to diversity and inclusion of underrepresented groups and climate change. Second, these social and environmental issues correspond to broader political values in society that are polarized along partisan lines; while liberal parties take prosocial and environmental positions, conservative parties utilize negative rhetoric around climate change and other controversial social issues (Inglehart & Norris, 2016). Therefore, we expect less strategic firm-level conditioning effect on employee political preferences over polarized social and environmental policy issues. We see organizational ideology as determined by an interaction between the employee's political values, the preexisting organizational ideology, and the strategic incentives of the firms.
We also scrutinize these explanations and consider our second hypothesis:
H2. Firms will place greater emphasis on social and environmental CSR due to ideological motivations.
sections.text:
Corporations engage in political actions through a variety of formal and informal activities. Formal corporate political activities include direct monetary contributions to electoral candidates or lobbying to gain access to legislature and influence regulatory frameworks.

These activities are generally aimed at shaping the regulatory environment, promoting favorable policy outcomes, or supporting politicians through institutionalized means. On the other hand, companies also engage in more direct public messaging through CSR and participation in ESG frameworks. These public messages convey firms' self-regulatory activities such as climate change adaptation and mitigation, as well as their stances on issues related to diversity and inclusion of marginalized and underrepresented groups. Through these frameworks, firms convey their positions on contentious and partisan social and environmental issues.

The rapidly rising number of CSR frameworks integrated into big businesses is a relatively new phenomenon (Steinberg, 2021). Globalization reduced barriers to trade and investment, and increased foreign direct investment has substantially widened the number of areas impacted by business operations. These operations and their social and environmental impacts caused a public backlash toward businesses. Non-governmental organizations and other civil society organizations pressured firms to take progressive action on social, economic, and environmental issues. Thus, CSR frameworks became a common feature of big business strategies (Detomasi, 2008). CSR broadly refers to a practice in which firms voluntarily provide social, environmental, and economic goods and services to communities affected by their operations (Steinberg, 2021). More specifically, a widely accepted definition posits that "CSR is the configuration of the principles of social responsibility, processes of social responsiveness, policies, programs, and observable outcomes as they relate to the firm's societal relationships" (Wood, 1991). CSR frameworks thus form an important but overlooked aspect of firms' political activities, as these frameworks often reflect firms' positions in larger political cleavages.

While CSR is ostensibly apolitical, some CSR directions, related to broader societal debates on race, gender, and the environment, are aligned more closely with the political left. In part, this pattern is the outcome of left-wing preferences of civil society and progressive organizations that pressure large corporations. Another aspect of this pattern is the widespread acceptance of traditionally liberal ideals, especially among high-income individuals representing important customer groups. This change impacts both the beliefs of employees and managers, as well as the societal expectations and constraints on company practices. While companies often pursue a variety of political strategies in less visible contexts, such as campaign contributions, CSR statements, and public communications generally emphasize issues associated with the left.

Existing literature provides some explanations for these seemingly costly strategies: (i) pragmatic motivations and (ii) ideological factors. Pragmatic motivations describe firms' incentives to countervail negative public image, advance their market share and profits, hedge sectoral and social risks, comply with regulatory/institutional pressures, and attract a skilled labor force (Briscoe & Safford, 2008;Detomasi, 2008;Midttun et al., 2007;Pacheco & Dean, 2015). First, in some cases, companies pursue CSR strategies to mitigate negative public backlash and countervail unpopular business practices through civil society initiatives. Firms thus pragmatically advance CSR strategies to minimize the impact of environmental or social prosecution/scandals on consumption patterns. For example, Greenpeace successfully pressured Shell to halt the deep-sea disposal of the Brent Spar oil platform and convinced Shell to dispose of it onshore (Detomasi, 2008). Similarly, negative public backlash toward footwear and apparel company Nike over its subcontractor production arrangements that resulted in poor working conditions has pressured the company to expand its CSR programs (Kytle & Ruggie, 2005). However, reactive CSR strategies are not systematic and are less likely to change long-run firm behavior. In other cases, companies that have already been targeted by formal criminal prosecution in various issue areas may introduce new ESG initiatives to neutralize the negative reputation cost between different audiences. These might be related to both short-and long-term reputation-building strategies.

Therefore, CSR strategies may sometimes go beyond reactive efforts to offset some negative effects of corporate prosecution/scandals and be directed toward meeting broader expectations among consumers and societal stakeholders (Bhagwat et al., 2020;O'Connor, 2006;Williams & Barrett, 2000). These strategies might build reputation, ease employee recruitment, and build customer loyalty (Jensen, 2002;Windsor, 2001). Inglehart and Norris (2016) and Norris and Inglehart (2019) highlight that postindustrial societies experienced a progressive value change, in which new secular values such as LGBT+ rights, environmentalism, open-mindedness toward migrants, and cosmopolitanism became salient, particularly among more educated younger generations. Therefore, firms strategically advance social and environmental CSR programs to build a positive public image to ease high-skilled employee recruitment. Companies also adopt CSR to comply with institutional and regulatory pressures (Mobus, 2012;Ngoasong, 2014). Indeed, these voluntary initiatives cannot be understood in separation from other regulatory threats (Grepperud & Pedersen, 2003). Detomasi (2008) and others argue that the political environment in which a firm operates has specific implications for the firm's choice of whether or how to pursue CSR (Aguilera-Caracuel et al., 2017;Dilling & Harris, 2018;Jindrichovska & Purcarea, 2011;Liu et al., 2014).

This political and regulatory landscape can sometimes incentivize firms to adopt politicized CSR strategies. An example of such a strategy is the launching of CSR initiatives by governments themselves, like targeted poverty alleviation campaigns. These campaigns, often supported by state-owned enterprises, illustrate a collaboration where political goals align with CSR. Firms, especially those like local enterprises or state-owned entities with strong ties to the government, are more likely to engage in these state-led CSR activities. This engagement is particularly evident in countries like China, where state-owned enterprises are recognized for assuming extensive social responsibilities (Deng et al., 2024;Huang et al., 2024). Furthermore, CSR/ESG programs can extend beyond consumer perceptions of the firm and also influence how the public feels about the regulatory policy context companies operate in (Culpepper et al., 2024;Kerner & Sumner, 2020;Malhotra et al., 2019). This implies that companies can strategically advance CSR initiatives to control societal expectations about potentially costly formal regulation or to be part of the design of policy initiatives. Formal regulations such as corporate income taxes may be costly for firms as they lower a firm's capital investment and its total factor productivity by raising the cost of capital. They will reduce posttax investment returns and decrease the profitability of businesses (Cevik & Miryugin, 2022). Therefore, managers have incentives to evade taxes and other regulatory initiatives such as environmental policies ranging from carbon taxes to compliance fees.

Moreover, these long-term considerations should also be more salient for companies suffering from negative reputational costs due to recent criminal prosecution. This suggests that companies that have been frequent and recent targets of criminal prosecution are more likely to publicly dedicate themselves to providing social and environmental goods. If unaccounted for, these negative public sentiments can escalate into broad public demands for corporate regulation. Similarly, certain industries that collectively suffer from poor reputations due to the extractive nature of their operations have more pragmatic incentives to communicate ESG efforts to pre-empt potentially costly public regulation (Malhotra et al., 2019).

Second, firms can also advance self-regulatory CSR mechanisms on social and environmental issues for competitive reasons. This type of pragmatic engagement is particularly salient for environmental issues, as the energy transition opens up new competitive market spaces for green industries, such as renewable energy and sustainable agriculture industries. The existing literature has pointed out various pragmatic motivations for companies to lobby in favor of stringent environmental standards or advance CSR/ESG initiatives. These include maximizing market share, achieving first-mover advantage, and adjusting to regulatory environments (Aguilera-Caracuel et al., 2017;Dong & Xu, 2016;Genovese, 2021;Genovese & Tvinnereim, 2019;Kennard, 2020;Liu et al., 2014;Porter & van der Linde, 1995;Vogel, 1997). Firms may thus expect that stances taken on social and environmental issues as part of a CSR strategy may contribute to overall higher profits and firm value (Bashtovaya, 2014;Ekatah et al., 2011;Midttun et al., 2007;Plumlee et al., 2015;Pätäri et al., 2014;Sepúlveda & Mendizabal, 2011). On the other hand, in some cases government-introduced mandatory CSR disclosures decrease the profitability of firms. These effects are particularly salient for state-owned enterprises and environmental sectors. This evidence suggests that CSR spending is primarily driven by social and political factors such as state ownership and polluting industries (Chen et al., 2018). In line with the literature on pragmatic motivations of firms for taking public political stances through CSR initiatives, we consider the following hypotheses:

H1. Firms will place greater emphasis on social and environmental CSR due to pragmatic motivations.

The literature on pragmatic motivations has mostly focused on how companies react to their external environment and highlighted factors related to profitability, market share, hedging risk, compliance with regulatory/institutional frameworks, and actions of industry peers to explain why firms behave as social and environmental stewards. Nonetheless, pragmatic motivations do not adequately capture why companies take public political positions on politically controversial issues that may not necessarily reflect concerns over profitability and other market-related mechanisms. Other research thus emphasizes ideological factors related to the internal characteristics of corporations such as the political action committees (PACs) (Bonica, 2016), the ideological orientation of the executives and the CEOs (Chin et al., 2013), and the organizational ideology of the firm and partisan preferences of its employees (Endres & Panagopoulos, 2017;Gupta et al., 2017;Maks-Solomon & Drewry, 2021;Newman & Bartels, 2011).

A relatively nascent literature focused on alternative factors that go beyond market-based explanations has started to investigate ideological factors that may contribute to the firms' decisions to make public political statements on controversial social and environmental issues. Indeed, socially responsible firms engage in fewer tax avoidance practices (Lanis & Richardson, 2013), and existing voluntary practices such as the donation culture discourage tax avoidance (Ge et al., 2023). However, we know relatively less about how factors like political ideology within the firms affect the emergence of voluntary firm initiatives such as the CSR and ESG programs.

Therefore, some scholars have shifted the attention toward high-rank executives, managers, and top decision-makers ideologies to understand various forms of CPA (Bonica, 2016;Chin et al., 2013). Indeed, some argue that any firm-level explanation is incomplete without accounting for the preferences of the firm's leadership. Recent research found that individual contributions by corporate elites reflect more partisan ideological preferences than typically assumed. Indeed, PACs behave more strategically than ideologically while engaging in political activity compared to individual contributions (Bonica, 2016). Nonetheless, even PACs need to adapt their strategies to the partisan and ideological preferences of their donors/employees (Li, 2018) Other research on ideological motivations has investigated the role of political ideologies of managers and business leaders independent of the political agendas of PACs (Chin et al., 2013;Francia et al., 2005;Tetlock, 2000). Individuals, including people employed in both mid and top-level positions, engage in political activities primarily due to their personal partisan preferences. Tetlock (2000), using a firm-level managerial survey, found that there are significant differences between liberal and conservative managers. Liberal managers are more likely to support firms' societal engagements as they value multiple stakeholders, whereas conservative managers value property rights more and prefer governance models that prioritize shareholder wealth. A more recent study focusing on CEOs' partisan political preferences found that liberal CEOs will advance CSR more than conservative CEOs (Chin et al., 2013).

While the focus on top executives and CEOs is useful for understanding CPA, top executives do not select corporate political activity solely on their own. Instead, they also have incentives to abide by pre-existing organizational ideology and other initiatives endorsed by employees at various organizational levels, enhancing the legitimacy of the CEOs within the firm (Suchman, 1995). It is plausible that other ideological factors related to the entire organizational body of the firm may explain the CPA and advancements in social and environmental CSR initiatives (Gupta et al., 2017;Maks-Solomon & Drewry, 2021).

Organizational political ideology broadly refers to prevailing political beliefs among an organization's members. While few firms have employees with homogeneous political beliefs, a certain degree of homogeneity is expected as individuals select places where the current members and policies attract them. When these types of homogenizations arise, they tend to be self-reinforcing and consequential in shaping certain types of CPA such as CSR (Gupta et al., 2017;Schneider, 1987). Organizational political ideology can guide policy-making within the firm through motivated cognition and logic of appropriateness. Motivated cognition refers to individuals' proclivity to reflect their values in their decisions. In addition, employees will also consider how their values match their understanding of the organization's prevailing ideology (logic of appropriateness). These two mechanisms are likely to enhance each other and can serve as the link between ideology, policy, and content of CSR initiatives (Gupta et al., 2017;Maks-Solomon & Drewry, 2021).

It is also important to note that firm-level characteristics may also affect individuals' political preferences on specific policy issues. A rich political economy literature focuses on occupational status and employer characteristics to explain political preferences on a wide range of policy issues including redistribution, trade, and foreign investment (Iversen & Soskice, 2001;Kitschelt & Rehm, 2014;Owen & Johnston, 2017;Scheve & Slaughter, 2004, 2001). In addition to sector-level effects, scholars have also emphasized that the size of the firm and the relative position of the employees within the firm could explain employee's political preferences over support for free trade. The evidence suggests that the employees of more productive firms are more likely to support free trade as they expect to benefit from the firm's success in some way (Lee & Liou, 2022). Indeed, Stuckatz (2022) shows that donations of the corporate PACs and employees are correlated with each other. However, there is also counterevidence suggesting that even the access-seeking corporate PACs are constrained by the political ideology of their employees/donors (Li, 2018).

Nonetheless, the distributional consequences of some issues such as free trade, redistribution, and investment might be relatively easier to identify for employees compared to social and environmental policies related to diversity and inclusion of underrepresented groups and climate change. Second, these social and environmental issues correspond to broader political values in society that are polarized along partisan lines; while liberal parties take prosocial and environmental positions, conservative parties utilize negative rhetoric around climate change and other controversial social issues (Inglehart & Norris, 2016). Therefore, we expect less strategic firm-level conditioning effect on employee political preferences over polarized social and environmental policy issues. We see organizational ideology as determined by an interaction between the employee's political values, the preexisting organizational ideology, and the strategic incentives of the firms.

We also scrutinize these explanations and consider our second hypothesis:

H2. Firms will place greater emphasis on social and environmental CSR due to ideological motivations.

sections.paper_id: Gur and Tomashevskiy - 2024 - Taking sides Corporate social responsibility and political ideology
paragraphs: To evaluate these hypotheses, we use CSR statements released by Fortune 1000 firms. These statements are useful in evaluating our hypotheses since CSR statements are designed to influence public perceptions of the firm's activities and place in the larger society. To this end, CSR statements often contain statements that are reflective of a firm's broader ideology. While these are not necessarily explicitly political statements, a firm's decision to emphasize some issues and de-emphasize others may be interpreted as an indication of a firm's broader political orientation. Whether acting pragmatically or ideologically, firms may signal their stance on political issues by devoting more or less space in their CSR statements to topics that align with conservative or liberal ideology. As an example, a firm that discusses in its CSR statement at length its commitment to diversity, inclusion, prolabor, and proenvironmental policies can be reasonably interpreted as emphasizing left-wing/liberal policies, when compared to a firm that allocates less space to such issues or emphasizes issues closer to right-wing/conservative ideological spectrum. 1 We collected CSR/ESG statements released by US Fortune 1000 companies in 2022. To analyze the content of these statements, we use semisupervised topic modeling. Topic modeling is a useful tool for analyzing text documents such as CSR statements, and for extracting topics with substantive relevance to our question. However, topic modeling results can be sensitive to the preset number of topics. Topics generated via traditional unsupervised topic modeling approaches, such as LDA models, may also be difficult to interpret substantively. To ensure that we are able to recover specific topics of interest, we use the keyword-assisted topic modeling approach (Eshima et al., 2023). 2 This approach permits analysts to specify keywords that a given topic of interest should contain. The model then generates topics that include the provided keywords, in addition to other keywords selected by the model, as well as other topics identified in the data. The keyword-assisted approach combines the exploratory features of traditional, unsupervised learning with a targeted, semisupervised approach to facilitate concept measurement. The keyword-assisted approach thus provides more confidence concerning the substantive content of the estimated topics.

We are interested in capturing language in CSR statements that correspond to social and environmental issues. To this end, we use the following keywords for our social and environmental topics. Social: community, diversity, social, governance, diverse, stakeholder. Environmental: emission, climate, sustainability, environment, gas, renewable. 3 In addition to the two keyword-assisted topics, we also estimate eight nonkeyword topics. For each firm's CSR document, we calculate the percentage of the document that belongs to either the social or the environmental topic and use these percentages as our dependent variables (DVs).

Figures 1 and 2 present some descriptive information about the keywords and estimated topics. 4 Figure 1 displays the frequency of these keywords in the CSR documents. This graph indicates that our selected keywords occur with some regularity across documents, improving confidence in topics generated with these keywords. Figure 2 presents the 10 estimated topics and the most common words associated with each topic. This graph also shows the average document share that belongs to each topic. The graph indicates that our keyword-assisted topics are consistent with key substantive concepts: both social and environmental CSR topics include specified keywords among the common words, and the words generally correspond well to substantive features of left-leaning social and environmental positions. These topics are also important in CSR documents: the social CSR topic is the second largest among all estimated topics, while the environmental topic is the sixth largest. Figure 3 plots the distributions of document proportions belonging to the social and environmental topics. This graph demonstrates that companies generally devote more space to discussing social issues, while environmental issues generally receive less attention. This result is intuitive since only companies operating in select sectors (energy, mining, etc.) would need to address environmental issues in their CSR statements. On the other hand, any company, regardless of sector, can communicate a position on social issues. To evaluate our hypotheses, we use two independent variables (IVs) to capture pragmatic and ideological motivations. For our "pragmatism" variable, we use data on the criminal prosecution of firms. 5 As identified in the existing literature, a pragmatic motivation for social or environmental messaging may involve a strategy to deflect away attention from damaging news and/or improve social standing in the broader community. We thus focus on the most likely case: if pragmatic factors related to public image matter, they should be most salient for corporate criminal prosecutions. If the pragmatic expectation is correct, we should expect firms that were targets of criminal prosecution to devote more space in CSR statements to social and environmental issues.

We use the corporate prosecution data in two ways: first, we use a binary variable coded to 1 for all firms that were prosecution targets in the period covered by our data (2004)(2005)(2006)(2007)(2008)(2009)(2010)(2011)(2012)(2013)(2014)(2015)(2016)(2017)(2018)(2019)(2020)(2021)(2022). We then disaggregate prosecution and create dummy variables for the types of various criminal offenses that triggered the prosecution. These offenses are Foreign Corrupt Practices Act (FCPA) and Food and Drug Administration (FDA) violations, Bribery, General Fraud, Securities Fraud, Antitrust, Environmental, Immigration, Workplace Safety, and Wildlife. Given the expectations in H1, we expect these variables to be positively associated with our DVs. Figure 4 displays the distribution of various types of prosecutions across firms' economic sectors. While firms in the manufacturing sector account for a large share of all prosecutions, firms in other sectors have been targets of criminal prosecution as well. We also present additional results in the appendix using different time lags for the corporate prosecution cases.

To measure ideological factors, we use data on political campaign contributions. 6 Using aggregate data on donations from firm employees and managers to US political candidates in the 2020 electoral cycle, we calculate the percentage of a firm's total contributions going to candidates from the Democratic party. This variable can be used as a measure of a firm's aggregate political

orientation, where firms with more liberal employees managers are more likely to donate to Democratic party candidates. We use donation data from 2020 because of the presidential election taking place in that year. Individuals are more likely to mobilize and participate in politics during a presidential election year, which should increase the coverage and reliability of data. 7 Our expectation stated in H2 is that firms with larger shares of contributions to Democrats will tend to devote a larger share of the CSR statements to social and environmental issues. Figure 5 displays the distribution of the variable in our sample. This distribution is somewhat trimodal: while a large number of firms are balanced in terms of the donation activities of their employees, a sizeable portion of firms are skewed in a liberal or conservative direction.

We use several additional controls to account for alternative factors that may influence our DVs. First, we account for company size using three variables: number of employees, total assets, and total revenue. We log these variables to account for outliers. These data were drawn from the Compustat database. We also include a company's Fortune 1000 rank as an additional control. Additionally, we include a variable equal to 1 if the firm had a female CEO in 2022. Firms with female CEOs may have a more liberal orientation and we include the variable as an additional measure of ideology. We also control for a firm's economic sector using two-digit North American Industry Classification System industries.

Using these DVs and IVs, we estimate a series of linear models using ordinary least squares (OLS). Although our DV is a percentage bounded between 0 and 1, OLS may predict values outside of these bounds. To address this issue, we also present results from a series of beta regression models, which are more appropriate for modeling variables bounded between 0 and 1. We present both sets of results to increase confidence in the robustness of our estimates since OLS can still be used to produce unbiased estimates of the conditional mean. Furthermore, since beta regression models are estimated using MLE, this can cause potential problems when estimating fixed effects models due to the incidental parameters problem. OLS does not suffer from these issues and can be used to estimate unbiased fixed effects estimates. Tables 1 and 2 F I G U R E 5 Democratic donations distribution.

present results from models for social and environmental CSR topics. 8 We include sector dummy variables when estimating models 3, 4, 7, and 8 in both tables. These results provide some strong evidence for H2 and weaker evidence for H1. For social CSR, the Democratic donations (dem. donations) variables are consistently positive and significant. This effect persists for both OLS and beta regression estimates. Overall, this suggests that firms with more liberal employees and managers tend to communicate a more liberal political message in their CSR statements. Interestingly, this effect persists for social CSR only; preference for Democratic candidates is consistently negatively associated with environmental CSR. This finding reveals an interesting relationship between social and environmental issues, indicating that these issues are substitutes, not complements. Firms thus reduce their emphasis on environmental issues as they become more liberal and focus more on social issues.

The evidence for H1 and the pragmatic motivation is less consistent. Corporate prosecution variables are significant in models 1, 2, 5, and 6 in Tables 1 and 2. When sector controls are included, both the aggregate prosecution variable and many of the disaggregated prosecution variables lose their significance. This suggests that sectoral factors are likely playing a role as a confounder: sectoral differences explain both exposures to types of prosecution as well as varying contents of CSR statements. However, when sectors are controlled for, some prosecution variables still retain significance. For social CSR OLS estimates, antitrust is negative and significant. For environmental CSR, the coefficient for immigration is negative and significant. These findings suggest that nonideological factors also affect public messaging, but in a manner that is inconsistent with prior expectations, since firms reduce environmental messaging in the wake of these scandals.

To further evaluate the role of sectoral effects, we plot average values for social and environmental CSR and the liberal orientation of companies across economic sectors in Figure 6. This figure provides some additional context for the results in Tables 1 and 2. Consistent with our results, companies in the energy sector tend to be more conservative, while also devoting the most attention to environmental CSR. This pattern points to energy firms' pragmatic incentive to address factors related to climate change through a public commitment to environmental CSR, combined with an ideological motivation to minimize social CSR. It is important to note that the substitution pattern of environmental and social CSR is not driven by the energy sector alone, as this graph and our results from models with sector-fixed effects show. In general, even if the energy sector is excluded, more liberal sectors place less emphasis on environmental CSR. Figure 6 further illustrates the relationship between social CSR and ideology, as more liberal sectors tend to emphasize social CSR.
In sum, these results provide consistent evidence in favor of H2, with weaker evidence for H1. When firms take positions on social or environmental issues, these positions appear to be

strongly influenced by the political preferences and orientation of firms' employees and managers. While pragmatic motivations do matter for some firms, as in the energy sector, these factors work in tandem with ideological factors. To the extent that firms take more partisan positions on social or political issues, these stances are not pragmatic or opportunistic actions but rather the outcomes of stable ideological preferences.

These results provide some strong evidence for H2 and weaker evidence for H1. For social CSR, the Democratic donations (dem. donations) variables are consistently positive and significant. This effect persists for both OLS and beta regression estimates. Overall, this suggests that firms with more liberal employees and managers tend to communicate a more liberal political message in their CSR statements. Interestingly, this effect persists for social CSR only; preference for Democratic candidates is consistently negatively associated with environmental CSR. This finding reveals an interesting relationship between social and environmental issues, indicating that these issues are substitutes, not complements. Firms thus reduce their emphasis on environmental issues as they become more liberal and focus more on social issues.

The evidence for H1 and the pragmatic motivation is less consistent. Corporate prosecution variables are significant in models 1, 2, 5, and 6 in Tables 1 and 2. When sector controls are included, both the aggregate prosecution variable and many of the disaggregated prosecution variables lose their significance. This suggests that sectoral factors are likely playing a role as a confounder: sectoral differences explain both exposures to types of prosecution as well as varying contents of CSR statements. However, when sectors are controlled for, some prosecution variables still retain significance. For social CSR OLS estimates, antitrust is negative and significant. For environmental CSR, the coefficient for immigration is negative and significant. These findings suggest that nonideological factors also affect public messaging, but in a manner that is inconsistent with prior expectations, since firms reduce environmental messaging in the wake of these scandals.

To further evaluate the role of sectoral effects, we plot average values for social and environmental CSR and the liberal orientation of companies across economic sectors in Figure 6. This figure provides some additional context for the results in Tables 1 and 2. Consistent with our results, companies in the energy sector tend to be more conservative, while also devoting the most attention to environmental CSR. This pattern points to energy firms' pragmatic incentive to address factors related to climate change through a public commitment to environmental CSR, combined with an ideological motivation to minimize social CSR. It is important to note that the substitution pattern of environmental and social CSR is not driven by the energy sector alone, as this graph and our results from models with sector-fixed effects show. In general, even if the energy sector is excluded, more liberal sectors place less emphasis on environmental CSR. Figure 6 further illustrates the relationship between social CSR and ideology, as more liberal sectors tend to emphasize social CSR.

sections.preview_head: To evaluate these hypotheses, we use CSR statements released by Fortune 1000 firms. These statements are useful in evaluating our hypotheses since CSR statements are designed to influence public perceptions of the firm's activities and place in the larger society. To this end, CSR statements often contain statements that are reflective of a firm's broader ideology. While these are not necessarily explicitly political statements, a firm's decision to emphasize some issues and de-emphasize others may be interpreted as an indication of a firm's broader political orientation.

sections.paper_id: Gur and Tomashevskiy - 2024 - Taking sides Corporate social responsibility and political ideology
paragraphs: In this paper, we examined some of the determinants of a firm's public political stances. We examined two explanations, rooted in pragmatic and ideological factors using a novel dataset of Fortune 1000 firms' CSR/ESG statements. Our results provide robust evidence for ideological determinants of CSR. We find that companies with more liberal employees and managers are more likely to emphasize social issues in their CSR statements. Conversely, the effects of recent

corporate prosecution on social CSR are less robust across different model specifications. These results provide stronger evidence in favor of ideological explanations compared to pragmatic motivations. However, on the environmental CSR side, the results are somewhat counterintuitive. We find that democratic donations are negatively associated with engagement with environmental CSR. In addition, we also find that carbon-intensive sectors are more likely to emphasize environmental topics in their CSR statements. These suggest that social and environmental messages are substitutes rather than complements and that political ideology is more important in firms' decisions to convey left-leaning social messages.

Our paper makes two important contributions to the literature on CPA. First, we provide a new text-based measure to study CPA. This is particularly important as more and more companies integrate CSR/ESG practices into their businesses and increasingly take part in politically controversial social and environmental debates through these documents. Therefore, we provide an innovative measure to understand why companies decide to convey political messages through these initiatives. Second, our results contribute to the existing explanations of CPA. We suggest that CPA is not only motivated by profit-seeking activity but also by political ideology. This finding holds broader significance for various forms of CPA and the role of firms in politics.

These findings have important implications for future research. While we do not distinguish between foreign and domestic firms in this project, it is unclear if there are differences in the political activity of multinational firms as compared to purely domestic companies. Future research ought to examine in more detail key variations in CPA between foreign and domestic firms. In this paper, we also use a more expansive notion of CPA. Researchers in future work should do more to analyze the various types of corporate engagement with politics and broaden the analytical scope to go beyond the research on lobbying and campaign contributions.

Finally, more research is necessary to understand the economic and financial consequences of CSR. How is the content of CSR statements linked to firms' economic and political activities? If firms are more likely to profit when engaging in "correct" CSR, ideological biases could cause firms to adopt "wrong" types of CSR, which may lead to adverse economic and regulatory outcomes. To answer these questions, additional data is necessary to understand the evolving content of CSR statements over time. Additional data to encompass various time-frames, additional firms, and a broader range of political activities is essential for a comprehensive examination of the impact of different forms of corporate political engagement. This expansion will provide a more nuanced understanding of how social or environmental CSR initiatives correlate with other activities and firms' financial performance. This type of data would also be instrumental in helping to further disentangle the interaction of ideological and pragmatic determinants of CSR.
